main_summary.summary: Michael D. McCarty, a Chicago-born storyteller (b. September 7, 1950), describes his West Side upbringing, Catholic education at Saint Ignatius College Prep, and entry into the Illinois chapter of the Black Panther Party. McCarty was interviewed by David Cline at his Los Angeles home on June 26, 2016, with Guha Shankar and John Bishop behind the camera. He recounts formative encounters with racial violence: a Crestwood Little League crowd that emptied the stands to chase him from a water fountain, and a near-execution beating by a South Side gang in which the gun misfired and a police officer refused to assist. McCarty cofounded a Black Student organization at Saint Ignatius with classmate Tyler, was expelled in March 1969 after organizing a walkout for Black studies, and received a diploma and apology from the school 39 years later in 2008 through organizer Greg Myers. He joined the Illinois chapter of the Black Panther Party at 2350 W. Madison Street in October-November 1968, served on the education cadre under Deputy Minister of Education Billy 'Che' Brooks, and worked with Deputy Chairman Fred Hampton until Hampton's 1969 assassination. McCarty discusses the Original Rainbow Coalition with the Young Patriots Organization and Young Lords, the FBI plant William O'Neal, and COINTELPRO operations.
main_summary.historical_significance:
McCarty provides a first-person account of the Illinois chapter of the Black Panther Party from the perspective of its education cadre, including direct testimony about Fred Hampton's organizing methods, the formation of the Original Rainbow Coalition with the Young Patriots Organization and the Young Lords, and the COINTELPRO infiltration that culminated in William O'Neal's role as FBI plant. The interview also documents the 2008 Saint Ignatius College Prep apology and posthumous diploma ceremony for Black students expelled in March 1969 for organizing a walkout, a discrete institutional reconciliation event organized by classmate Greg Myers.

McCarty's account adds specificity to the historical record on Chicago Black Panther political education practice, the cross-racial poor-people's organizing model that Hampton built across Black, Puerto Rican, and Appalachian-white constituencies, and the lived experience of growing up Black on Chicago's West Side adjacent to the sundown town of Cicero, Illinois.

chapters.title: Chicago Childhood and Family
chapters.summary: McCarty introduces himself, names his birthplace (Chicago, September 7, 1950), and describes growing up on the West Side after moving from the South Side at age three. He credits his mother as the greatest influence in his life, instilling a love of reading and the obligation to act when one sees something wrong.
related_events: McCarty family migration from South Side to West Side Chicago

chapters.title: Cicero and Crestwood Encounters
chapters.summary: McCarty describes growing up near the sundown town of Cicero, Illinois, where being Black after dark could mean harm or death. He recounts riding bicycles with friends for cycling merit badges in the Boy Scouts and being chased from a water fountain in Crestwood after a Little League crowd emptied the stands to attack him.
related_events: Crestwood Little League incident
Cicero gas station incident

chapters.title: South Side Beating and Police
chapters.summary: In his sophomore year, McCarty and a friend went to a party in a South Side high-rise project, where a gang beat them until tired. A gunman put a pistol to McCarty's head; a girl named in the moment as 'an angel' deflected the gun and the firearm misfired. A white police officer he flagged down for help dismissed him and then drove him back to the scene before taking him to a police station where the officers laughed.
related_events: South Side Chicago gang assault on McCarty

chapters.title: Saint Ignatius Black Student Organization
chapters.summary: McCarty joined the NAACP Youth Council and cofounded a Black Student organization at Saint Ignatius College Prep with classmate Tyler. The school initially supported interracial discussion panels at other Catholic schools. McCarty attended Saint Ignatius from 1965 until being expelled in March 1969.
related_events: Saint Ignatius Black Student organization founding 1966-67

chapters.title: Walkout, Expulsion, and 2008 Apology
chapters.summary: When the school refused the Black student organization's demands for Black studies, community outreach, and more minority students, McCarty and Tyler organized a walkout that drew press coverage. McCarty's mother learned of the walkout from the evening news. Both students were expelled. Thirty-nine years later, in 2008, classmate Greg Myers organized a reunion at which the school apologized and granted McCarty and Tyler their high school diplomas.
related_events: Saint Ignatius walkout March 1969
Saint Ignatius diploma and apology ceremony 2008

chapters.title: Martin Luther King in Chicago
chapters.summary: McCarty recalls King's 1966 Chicago campaign. King stayed on Hamlin near Douglas Park, where McCarty's classmate Tyler greeted him in passing. McCarty quotes King's remark that he had dealt with racism throughout the South but had seen nothing like racism in Chicago. He describes a Bridgeport door-to-door summer job where a white classmate had to extract them after overhearing threats of bodily harm.
related_events: Chicago Freedom Movement 1966
King brick attack at Chicago march

chapters.title: Joining the Black Panther Party
chapters.summary: After expulsion McCarty became a full-time member of the Illinois chapter of the Black Panther Party. He had followed the Party from reading Ramparts magazine and the 1967 Sacramento Capitol protest. He attended his first political education class at the Illinois chapter office at 2350 W. Madison Street in October or November 1968, where he encountered Deputy Minister of Education Billy 'Che' Brooks, whom he had known from track at John Marshall Metropolitan High School. McCarty joined the education cadre.
related_events: Black Panther Party Sacramento Capitol protest 1967
Illinois chapter Black Panther Party founding

chapters.title: Fred Hampton's Leadership
chapters.summary: McCarty describes Deputy Chairman Fred Hampton, who was 21 years old when assassinated. Hampton had organized as a high school student in Maywood, Illinois to integrate the public pool. McCarty recounts Hampton's response to a friend who came to the Party wanting a gun to kill white people: Hampton refused, gave him literature, and told him to return if still interested. McCarty notes that Fred sometimes sent him in his place for debates and speeches.
related_events: Fred Hampton Maywood pool integration organizing
Fred Hampton assassination December 1969

chapters.title: Original Rainbow Coalition
chapters.summary: McCarty describes the Original Rainbow Coalition that Hampton and the Illinois chapter built with the Young Patriots Organization (poor Appalachian whites) and the Young Lords (a Puerto Rican street gang that became political). Field secretaries Bob Lee and Hank 'Bull' Gaddis organized in the Appalachian-white community. McCarty references the documentary American Revolution 2 by Mike Gray showing a community meeting and a subsequent incident where Young Patriots surrounded a police car to free Bob Lee.
related_events: Original Rainbow Coalition formation 1969
American Revolution 2 documentary filming

chapters.title: COINTELPRO and Gang Outreach
chapters.summary: McCarty discusses the Black Panther Party's outreach to street gangs and meetings between Fred Hampton, Bobby Rush, and the Black P. Stone Nation. He describes COINTELPRO disinformation operations designed to disrupt the cross-racial coalition and the gang alliance, including anonymous letters and calls planted to provoke violence. He identifies William O'Neal, head of security, as the FBI plant.
related_events: COINTELPRO operations against Illinois Black Panther Party

chapters.title: The Burglary and Jean Seberg Case
chapters.summary: McCarty references The Burglary by Betty Medsger, on the peace activists who broke into the FBI office in Media, Pennsylvania on March 8, 1971 and exposed COINTELPRO. He recounts the case of an unnamed Los Angeles actress (Jean Seberg) whose pregnancy J. Edgar Hoover ordered the LA FBI office to undermine through planted rumors that the baby was not her husband's; the baby died and the actress later took her own life. McCarty states the LA FBI office was compensated for the operation.
related_events: Media Pennsylvania FBI office burglary March 8 1971
FBI Seberg disinformation operation

chapters.title: Free Breakfast and Community Programs
chapters.summary: McCarty describes the Free Breakfast for Children Program and the People's Free Medical Clinics, citing J. Edgar Hoover's identification of the Breakfast Program as the most dangerous Black Panther Party activity. He frames subsequent federal nutrition programs as a government response to Panther community organizing. The chapter ends mid-sentence as McCarty begins to describe Illinois chapter programs in the context of a 40th-anniversary reunion in Oakland.
related_events: Black Panther Party Free Breakfast for Children Program
Black Panther Party 40th anniversary reunion Oakland

loc_video.caption: Michael D. McCarty oral history interview conducted by David P. Cline in Los Angeles, California, 2016 June 26
